seo.title: Amazon Bedrock Agents Guardrails for Cybersecurity
seo.description: Compliance guide for Amazon Bedrock Agents agents in cybersecurity. Meet NIST Cybersecurity Framework, SOC 2 Type II requirements with Vex guardrails.

content.intro: Cybersecurity teams deploying Amazon Bedrock Agents face heightened risks when handling threat intelligence, automated incident response, and sensitive CVE data. Without robust compliance guardrails, agents can leak classified threat data via malformed Lambda responses or fail to enforce human approval for critical actions, violating NIST CSF and CISA directives. Vex provides verifiable post-processing for invoke_agent() calls to ensure regulatory compliance and prevent adversary exploitation.

complianceRequirements.description: Requires protection of threat intelligence data and controlled access to automated response mechanisms in AI systems.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex verifies agent outputs block PII/threat data leakage and enforce human-in-loop approval signals before action group execution.

complianceRequirements.description: Mandates audit trails for AI decision-making and prevention of unauthorized data exposure in enterprise deployments.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex creates immutable audit logs of agent sessions with policy violation evidence for SOC 2 evidence collection.

complianceRequirements.description: Requires risk assessment of AI systems handling classified security data with defined access controls.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex enforces domain-specific guardrails that catch policy violations before agent responses reach production systems.

complianceRequirements.description: Mandates human oversight for AI-generated security advisories and automated incident response actions.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex policy checks require explicit human approval flags in agent memory before authorizing critical actions.

industryRisks.name: Threat Intelligence Leakage
industryRisks.description: Agent action groups return unfiltered threat IOCs to LLM APIs, exposing them to adversaries.
industryRisks.example: Bedrock Agent queries knowledge base for APT41 indicators, Lambda returns full IP lists visible in API traces.

industryRisks.name: Unauthorized Automated Response
industryRisks.description: Agents trigger incident response actions without human approval, causing outages.
industryRisks.example: Agent auto-blocks legitimate IPs during DDoS false positive, disrupting customer services.

industryRisks.name: Unverified CVE Advisories
industryRisks.description: AI-generated security advisories contain inaccurate CVE references not validated against databases.
industryRisks.example: Agent recommends patching non-existent CVE-2024-12345, wasting engineering resources.

industryRisks.name: Knowledge Base Chunking Failures
industryRisks.description: Poor chunking misses critical threat documents, leading to incomplete incident analysis.
industryRisks.example: Ransomware TTPs split across chunks, agent fails to correlate attack patterns.

implementation.title: Wrap invoke_agent() with Vex Verification
implementation.description: Post-process all Bedrock Agent responses to catch malformed Lambda outputs and enforce cybersecurity policies.

implementation.title: TypeScript Agent Guardrail Verification
implementation.description: Verify multi-turn session state and block unverified CVE references in agent responses.

implementation.title: Knowledge Base Response Validation
implementation.description: Ensure KB retrieval contains minimum relevant documents and no PII before agent processing.

caseStudyScenario.heading: Amazon Bedrock Agents in Cybersecurity: A Scenario
caseStudyScenario.scenario: A global MSSP deploys Bedrock Agents to accelerate SOC triage, integrating threat intelligence knowledge bases with action groups that query SIEM systems and generate CVE advisories. During a live ransomware incident, the agent retrieves APT29 TTPs but Lambda functions return unfiltered IOCs including classified sinkhole IPs. The agent then auto-generates a response recommending firewall blocks without human approval, while chunking issues cause it to miss critical C2 infrastructure docs split across retrieval boundaries. Without guardrails, this leaks intelligence to monitoring adversaries and risks blocking legitimate traffic.
caseStudyScenario.outcome: Vex guardrails wrapped around invoke_agent() immediately flagged the IOC leakage (3 violations), blocked the unapproved auto-remediation, and flagged missing KB context. The SOC analyst received verified threat data with explicit approval prompts, cross-referenced CVEs against NIST NVD via Vex policy checks, and safely contained the incident. Post-incident audit showed 100% compliance with NIST CSF PR.AC-4 and CISA AI directives, preventing $2.3M in potential damages from false positive response.

cta.heading: Comply with Cybersecurity Regulations Using Vex
cta.description: Integrate Vex guardrails in your Bedrock Agents today to prevent threat leakage and ensure NIST/CISA compliance.
